en.extract:
The bay woodpecker (Blythipicus pyrrhotis) is a species of bird in the family Picidae.
It is found in Bangladesh, Bhutan, Cambodia, China, Hong Kong, India, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Nepal, Thailand, and Vietnam. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest and subtropical or tropical moist montane forest.


Diet and nutrition
The bay woodpecker's diet consists of various insects such as ants, termites and wood-boring beetles, and they will also consume berries occasionally. They do most of their foraging 3–4 m below the surface level, on trunks, rotting snags, logs, and on saplings, vines and bamboo. They do not forage near other bay woodpeckers, but they maintain loose contact with their partners.


Description
The bay woodpecker is a mid-size bird with a body length of 26.5–30 cm (10.4–11.8 in). The average adult bay woodpecker has a weight of 126–170 g (4.4–6.0 oz). Male bay woodpeckers can be distinguished by a brown crown on their heads, a short crest and nape with streaking, and a pale brown forehead. Female bay woodpeckers have a shorter bill than the males, as well as a pale head without any red on the neck. Bay woodpeckers are members of the Picidae bird family which also includes piculets, wrynecks, and sapsuckers.


Song and call
The bay woodpecker has a long, dry rattle that they use as a contact between mates. Their territorial announcement can be heard over large distances.


Distribution and habitat
Bay woodpeckers typically live in evergreen and mixed deciduous forests. They are found in heavily wooded ravines with dense growth. Bay woodpeckers are found in a variety of altitudes, ranging from as low as 50 m, and as high as 2750 m. Bay woodpeckers are most commonly found in the following South Asian countries: Bangladesh, Bhutan, Cambodia, China, India, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Nepal, Thailand, and Vietnam.


Subspecies
There are 5 subspecies of bay woodpeckers:

Blythipicus pyrrhotis pyrrhotis: Eastern Nepal to south-central China, Laos, and northern Vietnam.
Blythipicus pyrrhotis sinensis: South-eastern China.
Blythipicus pyrrhotis annamensis: Southern Vietnam highlands.
Blythipicus pyrrhotis hainanus: Hainan, China
Blythipicus pyrrhotis cameroni: Malaysia


Conservation status
The bay woodpecker is not globally threatened, but they are generally uncommon. Their population is currently on the decline due to habitat loss, but is not expected to approach the threshold of Vulnerable under the population trend criterion. According to this criterion, they are classified under the "least concern" category of extinction and endangerment. The global population size is currently unknown, but the population of bay woodpeckers in China, one of their primary habitats, is estimated to be at 10,000-100,000 breeding pairs.


Breeding
Bay woodpeckers will use displays such as crest-raising and wing-flicking when calling for a mate. Both sexes will dig a nest hole that is low down (1–4 m) in live or dead wood. The parents will share incubation and brood-feeding.


References

The '''bay woodpecker''' ('''''Blythipicus pyrrhotis''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Picidae]].

It is found in [[Bangladesh]], [[Bhutan]], [[Cambodia]], [[China]], [[Hong Kong]], [[India]], [[Laos]], [[Malaysia]], [[Myanmar]], [[Nepal]], [[Thailand]], and [[Vietnam]]. Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]] and subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]].

== Diet and nutrition ==
The bay woodpecker's diet consists of various insects such as ants, [[termite]]s and [[Woodboring beetle|wood-boring beetles]], and they will also consume berries occasionally. They do most of their foraging 3–4 m below the surface level, on trunks, rotting snags, logs, and on saplings, vines and bamboo.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Winkler |first=Hans |last2=Christie |first2=David |date=2020 |title=Bay Woodpecker (Blythipicus pyrrhotis), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/baywoo1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.baywoo1.01|url-access=subscription }}</ref> They do not forage near other bay woodpeckers, but they maintain loose contact with their partners.<ref name=":0" />

== Description ==
The bay woodpecker is a mid-size bird with a body length of {{convert|26.5|–|30|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":0" /> The average adult bay woodpecker has a weight of {{convert|126|–|170|g|oz|abbr=on}}. Male bay woodpeckers can be distinguished by a brown crown on their heads, a short crest and [[nape]] with streaking, and a pale brown forehead.<ref name=":0" /> Female bay woodpeckers have a shorter bill than the males, as well as a pale head without any red on the neck.<ref name=":0" /> Bay woodpeckers are members of the Picidae bird family which also includes [[piculet]]s, [[wryneck]]s, and [[sapsucker]]s.<ref name=":0" />

== Song and call ==
The bay woodpecker has a long, dry rattle that they use as a contact between mates.<ref name=":0" /> Their territorial announcement can be heard over large distances.<ref name=":0" />

== Distribution and habitat ==
Bay woodpeckers typically live in [[evergreen]] and mixed [[Deciduous|deciduous forests]].<ref name=":0" /> They are found in heavily wooded [[ravine]]s with dense growth.<ref name=":0" /> Bay woodpeckers are found in a variety of [[altitude]]s, ranging from as low as 50 m, and as high as 2750 m.<ref name=":0" /> Bay woodpeckers are most commonly found in the following South Asian countries: [[Bangladesh]], [[Bhutan]], [[Cambodia]], [[China]], [[India]], [[Laos]], [[Malaysia]], [[Myanmar]], [[Nepal]], [[Thailand]], and [[Vietnam]].<ref name=":0" />

== Subspecies ==
There are 5 [[subspecies]] of bay woodpeckers:<ref name="BOTW">{{cite journal |last1=Winkler |first1=Hans |last2=Christie |first2=David |title=Bay Woodpecker (Blythipicus pyrrhotis) |journal=Birds of the World |date=4 March 2020 |doi=10.2173/bow.baywoo1.01}}</ref>

*''Blythipicus pyrrhotis pyrrhotis'': Eastern [[Nepal]] to south-central [[China]], [[Laos]], and northern [[Vietnam]].
*''Blythipicus pyrrhotis sinensis'': South-eastern China.
*''Blythipicus pyrrhotis annamensis'': Southern Vietnam highlands.
*''Blythipicus pyrrhotis hainanus'': [[Hainan|Hainan, China]]
*''Blythipicus pyrrhotis cameroni'': [[Malaysia]]

== Conservation status ==
The bay woodpecker is not globally threatened, but they are generally uncommon.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Bay Woodpecker (Blythipicus pyrrhotis) - BirdLife species factsheet |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/22681546 |access-date=2022-10-25 |website=datazone.birdlife.org}}</ref> Their population is currently on the decline due to habitat loss, but is not expected to approach the threshold of Vulnerable under the population trend criterion.<ref name=":2" /> According to this criterion, they are classified under the "least concern" category of [[extinction]] and endangerment.<ref name=":2" /> The global population size is currently unknown, but the population of bay woodpeckers in [[China]], one of their primary [[habitat]]s, is estimated to be at 10,000-100,000 [[breeding pair]]s.<ref name=":2" />

== Breeding ==
Bay woodpeckers will use displays such as crest-raising and wing-flicking when calling for a mate.<ref name=":0" /> Both sexes will dig a [[nest]] hole that is low down (1–4 m) in live or dead wood.<ref name=":0" /> The parents will share incubation and brood-feeding.<ref name=":0" />

zh.extract:
黄嘴噪啄木鸟（学名：Blythipicus pyrrhotis）为啄木鸟科噪啄木鸟属的鸟类。主要分布于印度東北部、尼泊爾、孟加拉、不丹、中國華南地區以及中南半島。主要栖息于亚热带或热带潮湿低地森林或热带潮湿山地森林。该物种的模式产地在尼泊尔。


亚种
黄嘴噪啄木鸟海南亚种（学名：Blythipicus pyrrhotis hainanus）。在中国大陆，分布于海南等地。该物种的模式产地在海南。
黄嘴噪啄木鸟指名亚种（学名：Blythipicus pyrrhotis pyrrhotis）。在中国大陆，分布于云南等地。该物种的模式产地在尼泊尔。
黄嘴噪啄木鸟华南亚种（学名：Blythipicus pyrrhotis sinensis）。在中国大陆，分布于四川、贵州、广西、广东、湖南、福建等地。该物种的模式产地在福建。
